volunteer_history.where_and_when: Evergreen Family Shelter (2020 - June 2024); Rainier Valley Community Learning Center (early 2017 - March 2020); Portland Community Food Share (2013 - 2016)
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_duties_line_1: Coordinating evening homework sessions for kids in transitional housing.
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_reason_for_leavin: Stepped back due to new role at HarborBridge.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_2_line_1: Helping adult learners with English through practical exercises.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_reason_for_leaving_2_l: Classes went virtual due to COVID, and faced connectivity issues.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_3_line_1: Involved in intake, helping families navigate their options with dignity.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_3_line_2: Trained new volunteers on food safety.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_reason_for_leaving_3_l: Moved to Seattle for a job at North Sound Data Solutions.

volunteer_history.where_and_when: I began volunteering regularly in college around 2010 at various campus and community organizations in Portland, Oregon, including food drives and neighborhood clean-ups. Since moving to Seattle in 2016, I have consistently volunteered with education- and housing-focused nonprofits, most notably with Rainier Valley Community Learning Center (2017–2020) and Evergreen Family Shelter (2020–2024). I typically commit to at least one ongoing weekly volunteer role per year, plus several one-off events such as fundraising runs, book drives, and community fairs.
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_your_title: Evening Homework Club Tutor & Program Assistant (Volunteer)
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_duties_line_1: Provided twice-weekly after-school homework support and academic mentoring to 8–12 elementary and middle school students living in transitional housing, focusing on reading comprehension, basic math skills, and building consistent study habits.
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_duties_line_2: Assisted the program coordinator with setting up and closing the homework room, tracking student attendance and progress in a shared spreadsheet, communicating with parents and shelter staff about student needs, coordinating small volunteer teams (3–4 volunteers per shift), and developing simple enrichment activities (STEM games, journaling prompts, and read-aloud sessions) tailored to children experiencing housing instability or trauma.
volunteer_history.current_or_most_recent_reason_for_leavin: Relocated to a different part of the city and started a new full-time job with longer evening hours, making it difficult to reliably attend the weekday homework sessions.

I discussed my changing schedule with the volunteer coordinator in early 2024, and we agreed that it would be better for the students to have a tutor who could commit to being there every week. I stayed on through the end of the school year (June 2024) to help transition my responsibilities, document the routines I had established for the reading groups, introduce the new volunteers to the students, and ensure that the children had continuity and did not feel abruptly abandoned. I remain on their mailing list and have offered to come back for occasional weekend events when my schedule allows.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_2_line_1: Supported an evening English-as-a-Second-Language (ESL) class for adult learners by circulating during small-group and pair activities, helping students practice conversation, pronunciation, and basic writing tasks such as filling out forms, drafting simple work emails, and preparing for job interviews.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_2_line_2: Assisted the lead instructor with classroom logistics, including preparing handouts, setting up audio equipment, taking attendance, and recording student participation notes; provided individualized support to learners with very limited literacy; occasionally led warm-up exercises and conversation circles focused on real-life topics like visiting the doctor, talking to a child’s teacher, or navigating public transportation; and offered informal tech help using translation apps and email on students’ phones when appropriate.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_reason_for_leaving_2_l: In-person classes were suspended in March 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and I was unable to continue regularly once the program shifted to a fully virtual model.

When the center moved classes online, many of the students I worked with did not have stable internet access or were sharing devices with multiple family members. My own work responsibilities also increased significantly when my employer transitioned to remote operations, and I was logging unpredictable hours. I helped the instructor for a few weeks with phone check-ins and basic Zoom orientations, but I could not commit to the full virtual schedule. After talking with the staff, we decided to formally end my weekly volunteer role while leaving the door open for future in-person opportunities, which eventually led me to shift my focus to the homework club at Evergreen Family Shelter once in-person youth programs resumed.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_3_line_1: Welcomed clients to the pantry, conducted brief intake screenings to verify eligibility based on income and household size, and helped families select appropriate food items while maintaining dignity, privacy, and respect for cultural and dietary needs.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_duties_3_line_2: Restocked shelves, sorted and checked donations for quality and expiration dates, assembled emergency food boxes for delivery to homebound seniors, assisted with weekly inventory counts, and helped train newer volunteers on pantry procedures, food safety basics, and how to de-escalate stressful situations when clients were anxious or frustrated due to long wait times or limited supplies.
volunteer_history.volunteer_history_reason_for_leaving_3_l: Moved from Portland, Oregon to Seattle, Washington for a new full-time job opportunity and could no longer attend weekly shifts at the pantry.

During my final month, I coordinated with the volunteer manager to gradually hand off my regular Thursday evening shift and update some of the intake instructions I had been helping to refine, so that newer volunteers would have clear written guidance. I left in good standing and have stayed in occasional contact with a former fellow volunteer through social media; I also still donate annually to support the pantry’s holiday food drive even though I no longer live in the area.

work_history.work_history_duties_1_line_1: Analyzed customer behavior and operational data for mid-sized nonprofit and education-sector clients, building dashboards and recurring reports in Tableau and Power BI to help them track program outcomes, donor engagement, and internal performance metrics; regularly cleaned and merged data from multiple systems (CRM, email marketing, and event registration platforms) using SQL and Python.
work_history.work_history_duties_1_line_2: Collaborated with cross-functional teams (account managers, software engineers, and client success staff) to translate non-technical client questions into clear analytical projects, presented findings to both technical and non-technical stakeholders, documented methodologies and data dictionaries, mentored two junior analysts on best practices for data quality and visualization design, and contributed to internal process improvements such as standardizing report templates for nonprofit clients.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_1_line_1: Accepted a new position with a smaller mission-driven analytics firm that focuses exclusively on supporting education and housing nonprofits, aligning more closely with my long-term career and volunteer interests.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_1_line_2: After five years at North Sound Data Solutions, I felt that I had grown significantly in my technical skills but wanted to move into a role where the majority of my client work directly supported organizations addressing educational equity and housing stability—the same areas where I have been volunteering. I discussed my goals with my manager during my annual review in early 2024, and when an opportunity arose at a specialized social-impact analytics firm, I applied and was offered the role. I provided over a month of notice, documented my active projects in detail, trained my replacement on key client accounts, and stayed on call for questions during the first two weeks after my departure to ensure a smooth transition for the clients I had worked with for several years.
work_history.work_history_duties_2_line_1: Supported senior researchers on program evaluation projects for local governments and nonprofits by conducting literature reviews, cleaning and coding survey data in SPSS and Excel, helping design questionnaires and interview protocols, and assisting with the preparation of draft reports and slide decks for client presentations.
work_history.work_history_duties_2_line_2: Coordinated logistics for focus groups and stakeholder interviews (scheduling, reminder calls, room setup, and note-taking), contributed to basic descriptive analyses and visualizations, maintained organized project files and version control for research instruments, and communicated regularly with clients to clarify data questions and follow up on missing information while adhering to confidentiality and data protection standards.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_2_line_1: Relocated from Portland to Seattle and sought a more advanced, analytics-focused role after completing several multi-year evaluation projects.
work_history.work_history_reason_for_leaving_2_line_2: By mid-2019, I had reached a natural transition point on two major projects that were wrapping up, and I had also decided to move to Seattle to be closer to family and pursue broader career opportunities. My supervisors were aware of my relocation plans several months in advance, and we coordinated my departure so that final reports and data handoffs were completed before my last day. They provided strong references that helped me secure my subsequent position at North Sound Data Solutions, and we remain in professional contact for occasional consulting questions and networking.

differences.ground_truth: Evening Homework Club Tutor & Program Assistant (Volunteer)

I began volunteering regularly in college around 2010 at various campus and community organizations in Portland, Oregon, including food drives and neighborhood clean-ups. Since moving to Seattle in 2016, I have consistently volunteered with education- and housing-focused nonprofits, most notably with Rainier Valley Community Learning Center (2017–2020) and Evergreen Family Shelter (2020–2024). I typically commit to at least one ongoing weekly volunteer role per year, plus several one-off events such as fundraising runs, book drives, and community fairs.
differences.generated: Evergreen Family Shelter (2020 - June 2024); Rainier Valley Community Learning Center (early 2017 - March 2020); Portland Community Food Share (2013 - 2016)

differences.ground_truth: Assisted the lead instructor with classroom logistics, including preparing handouts, setting up audio equipment, taking attendance, and recording student participation notes; provided individualized support to learners with very limited literacy; occasionally led warm-up exercises and conversation circles focused on real-life topics like visiting the doctor, talking to a child’s teacher, or navigating public transportation; and offered informal tech help using translation apps and email on students’ phones when appropriate.

Assisted the program coordinator with setting up and closing the homework room, tracking student attendance and progress in a shared spreadsheet, communicating with parents and shelter staff about student needs, coordinating small volunteer teams (3–4 volunteers per shift), and developing simple enrichment activities (STEM games, journaling prompts, and read-aloud sessions) tailored to children experiencing housing instability or trauma.
Seattle, WA 98118
1458 Maple Terrace

I discussed my changing schedule with the volunteer coordinator in early 2024, and we agreed that it would be better for the students to have a tutor who could commit to being there every week. I stayed on through the end of the school year (June 2024) to help transition my responsibilities, document the routines I had established for the reading groups, introduce the new volunteers to the students, and ensure that the children had continuity and did not feel abruptly abandoned. I remain on their mailing list and have offered to come back for occasional weekend events when my schedule allows.

Supported an evening English-as-a-Second-Language (ESL) class for adult learners by circulating during small-group and pair activities, helping students practice conversation, pronunciation, and basic writing tasks such as filling out forms, drafting simple work emails, and preparing for job interviews.
differences.generated: Helping adult learners with English through practical exercises.

differences.ground_truth: Adult ESL Classroom Assistant (Volunteer)

When the center moved classes online, many of the students I worked with did not have stable internet access or were sharing devices with multiple family members. My own work responsibilities also increased significantly when my employer transitioned to remote operations, and I was logging unpredictable hours. I helped the instructor for a few weeks with phone check-ins and basic Zoom orientations, but I could not commit to the full virtual schedule. After talking with the staff, we decided to formally end my weekly volunteer role while leaving the door open for future in-person opportunities, which eventually led me to shift my focus to the homework club at Evergreen Family Shelter once in-person youth programs resumed.

Restocked shelves, sorted and checked donations for quality and expiration dates, assembled emergency food boxes for delivery to homebound seniors, assisted with weekly inventory counts, and helped train newer volunteers on pantry procedures, food safety basics, and how to de-escalate stressful situations when clients were anxious or frustrated due to long wait times or limited supplies.
differences.generated: Trained new volunteers on food safety.

differences.ground_truth: In-person classes were suspended in March 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and I was unable to continue regularly once the program shifted to a fully virtual model.
differences.generated: Classes went virtual due to COVID, and faced connectivity issues.

differences.ground_truth: Food Pantry Intake & Distribution Volunteer

2275 4th Ave, Suite 900

Supported senior researchers on program evaluation projects for local governments and nonprofits by conducting literature reviews, cleaning and coding survey data in SPSS and Excel, helping design questionnaires and interview protocols, and assisting with the preparation of draft reports and slide decks for client presentations.

Coordinated logistics for focus groups and stakeholder interviews (scheduling, reminder calls, room setup, and note-taking), contributed to basic descriptive analyses and visualizations, maintained organized project files and version control for research instruments, and communicated regularly with clients to clarify data questions and follow up on missing information while adhering to confidentiality and data protection standards.

Collaborated with cross-functional teams (account managers, software engineers, and client success staff) to translate non-technical client questions into clear analytical projects, presented findings to both technical and non-technical stakeholders, documented methodologies and data dictionaries, mentored two junior analysts on best practices for data quality and visualization design, and contributed to internal process improvements such as standardizing report templates for nonprofit clients.
1015 SW Morrison St, Suite 430
Data Analyst
N/A

By mid-2019, I had reached a natural transition point on two major projects that were wrapping up, and I had also decided to move to Seattle to be closer to family and pursue broader career opportunities. My supervisors were aware of my relocation plans several months in advance, and we coordinated my departure so that final reports and data handoffs were completed before my last day. They provided strong references that helped me secure my subsequent position at North Sound Data Solutions, and we remain in professional contact for occasional consulting questions and networking.
Research Assistant

Accepted a new position with a smaller mission-driven analytics firm that focuses exclusively on supporting education and housing nonprofits, aligning more closely with my long-term career and volunteer interests.
Bridgeview Research & Consulting

After five years at North Sound Data Solutions, I felt that I had grown significantly in my technical skills but wanted to move into a role where the majority of my client work directly supported organizations addressing educational equity and housing stability—the same areas where I have been volunteering. I discussed my goals with my manager during my annual review in early 2024, and when an opportunity arose at a specialized social-impact analytics firm, I applied and was offered the role. I provided over a month of notice, documented my active projects in detail, trained my replacement on key client accounts, and stayed on call for questions during the first two weeks after my departure to ensure a smooth transition for the clients I had worked with for several years.

Analyzed customer behavior and operational data for mid-sized nonprofit and education-sector clients, building dashboards and recurring reports in Tableau and Power BI to help them track program outcomes, donor engagement, and internal performance metrics; regularly cleaned and merged data from multiple systems (CRM, email marketing, and event registration platforms) using SQL and Python.
Relocated from Portland to Seattle and sought a more advanced, analytics-focused role after completing several multi-year evaluation projects.
North Sound Data Solutions
Portland, OR 97205

event_info.special_needs_or_medical_conditions_we_s:
I don’t have any major medical conditions that would prevent me from fully participating in the conference, but there are a few minor things that may be helpful for the organizers to know in case of an emergency or when planning seating and room arrangements.

1) Mild seasonal allergies: I have mild seasonal allergies (primarily to grass and tree pollen). This usually just means I may sneeze a bit more if outdoor sessions or networking events are held in grassy areas, but it does not require any special medical support. I usually carry over-the-counter antihistamines (cetirizine) with me and take them as needed.

2) Sensitivity to very strong fragrances: Strong artificial fragrances (especially some perfumes and heavily scented cleaning products) can occasionally trigger mild headaches or light nausea for me. It’s not severe, but if possible I prefer to sit a bit away from heavily scented candles or diffusers. If there is a designated fragrance-free or low-scent area in conference rooms, I’d appreciate being able to sit there.

3) Food-related note (not an allergy): I do not have any food allergies, but I try to avoid large amounts of dairy during the daytime sessions because it tends to make me a bit sluggish and can occasionally upset my stomach. I’m fine with small amounts of cheese or milk in coffee, but I generally prefer non-dairy milk options (oat or almond) if they are available. This is more of a preference than a medical requirement, but it does help me stay more alert and engaged during long sessions.

4) General physical needs: I am able to walk and stand without assistance, but I do have a minor lower back issue from an old sports injury (nothing serious). If sessions involve prolonged standing, I may look for a chair or a place to sit occasionally. During longer plenary sessions, I really appreciate chairs with at least minimal back support. No special accommodations are strictly necessary, but I mention this in case there is any flexibility around seating or stretch breaks.

Overall, I’m fully able to participate in all activities, including the tours and regular conference schedule. These notes are just to give context in case there are options for seating areas, fragrance-light zones, or non-dairy beverage alternatives.
event_info.anything_else_we_should_know_about_you:
I’m attending as a representative of Whitaker Analytics Group, a small data consulting firm I founded in 2018 that focuses on helping mid-sized nonprofits and membership associations make better use of their data for decision-making. My background is in economics and statistics, and I spent about ten years working inside a national nonprofit before moving into consulting. Because of that, I tend to approach sponsorships like this as both a networking opportunity and a chance to genuinely support the community I’m working with.

A few personal details that might be useful for planning and context:

1) Professional interests: I’m especially interested in sessions related to data governance, responsible AI use for associations, and practical case studies about improving member engagement through analytics. If there are any breakout groups or roundtables on those topics, I’d be very eager to join or even contribute informally. I’ve done several workshops on using dashboards to tell stories to boards of directors, and I’m always happy to share slide decks or examples if that would be helpful.

2) Personality and networking style: I’m fairly outgoing once I’ve met people, but I tend to be a little quiet during the first hour of an event while I get my bearings. If there are any structured networking activities (e.g., small-group introductions, topic tables, or sponsor meet-and-greet sessions), those really help me connect with people more naturally. I also make a point of being approachable at our sponsor table—if you’re assigning table locations, I don’t mind being in a fairly high-traffic area because I actually enjoy answering questions from attendees.

3) Travel details: I’ll be flying in from Austin on Monday afternoon and staying at the main conference hotel through Thursday morning. That means I’ll be available for Tuesday and Wednesday daytime and evening events, but I’ll likely head out early on Thursday to catch my flight. If there are any sponsor briefings or logistics meetings, it’s easiest for me to attend them on Tuesday morning before sessions begin or during a mid-morning break.

4) Dietary preferences: As mentioned above, I try to go light on dairy during the day and prefer balanced meals with some protein and vegetables so I’m not falling asleep after lunch. I’m not vegetarian or vegan, but I’m very happy with vegetarian options. If there are buffet-style meals, I usually build a plate with lean protein, vegetables, and something light like rice or salad. I drink coffee in the morning (ideally with oat milk if available) and switch to tea or water in the afternoon.

5) Hobbies and conversation starters: Outside of work, I’m a long-distance runner and usually get up early to run a few miles around the city I’m visiting. If there are safe running paths near the hotel, any maps or local tips would be appreciated. I also enjoy local coffee shops and bookstores, so recommendations from staff or other attendees are always welcome. I mention this because I’ve found that casual conversations about running routes or local spots often turn into really meaningful professional connections.

6) Accessibility and comfort: I don’t require formal accessibility accommodations, but I do appreciate clear signage and printed schedules or maps. I tend to take notes by hand as well as on my laptop, so a table surface or at least a chair with an armrest is helpful. If there are any quiet corners or lounge areas where people can step away from the noise for a few minutes, I often use those between sessions to recharge.

7) Sponsorship expectations: My main goals as a sponsor are (a) to learn more about the challenges associations are facing around data and technology, (b) to meet potential partners and clients in a low-pressure setting, and (c) to support this event so it continues in future years. I’m not interested in hard selling; I prefer longer-term relationships where we can be a helpful resource even if someone never becomes a paying client. If there are any guidelines about how sponsors should engage with attendees (e.g., limits on email follow-ups, expectations around prize drawings or swag), I’d appreciate having those clearly laid out so we can align with the culture of the event.

If you need any additional details from me ahead of time—such as a short bio, logo files, or information about any materials we plan to bring for our table—please feel free to reach out by email. I try to respond within 24 hours on weekdays.

recall_failure_details.ground_truth:
I don’t have any major medical conditions that would prevent me from fully participating in the conference, but there are a few minor things that may be helpful for the organizers to know in case of an emergency or when planning seating and room arrangements.

1) Mild seasonal allergies: I have mild seasonal allergies (primarily to grass and tree pollen). This usually just means I may sneeze a bit more if outdoor sessions or networking events are held in grassy areas, but it does not require any special medical support. I usually carry over-the-counter antihistamines (cetirizine) with me and take them as needed.

2) Sensitivity to very strong fragrances: Strong artificial fragrances (especially some perfumes and heavily scented cleaning products) can occasionally trigger mild headaches or light nausea for me. It’s not severe, but if possible I prefer to sit a bit away from heavily scented candles or diffusers. If there is a designated fragrance-free or low-scent area in conference rooms, I’d appreciate being able to sit there.

3) Food-related note (not an allergy): I do not have any food allergies, but I try to avoid large amounts of dairy during the daytime sessions because it tends to make me a bit sluggish and can occasionally upset my stomach. I’m fine with small amounts of cheese or milk in coffee, but I generally prefer non-dairy milk options (oat or almond) if they are available. This is more of a preference than a medical requirement, but it does help me stay more alert and engaged during long sessions.

4) General physical needs: I am able to walk and stand without assistance, but I do have a minor lower back issue from an old sports injury (nothing serious). If sessions involve prolonged standing, I may look for a chair or a place to sit occasionally. During longer plenary sessions, I really appreciate chairs with at least minimal back support. No special accommodations are strictly necessary, but I mention this in case there is any flexibility around seating or stretch breaks.

Overall, I’m fully able to participate in all activities, including the tours and regular conference schedule. These notes are just to give context in case there are options for seating areas, fragrance-light zones, or non-dairy beverage alternatives.

expedited_review.explanation_required_expedite:
Requesting expedited review due to rapidly worsening functional status and high fall risk in a frail 76-year-old Medicare Advantage member.

Over the past 6 months, Ms. Martha Henderson has experienced a marked decline in mobility and independence secondary to severe osteoarthritis of the left knee. She now reports that she can walk only about half a block, even with a cane, before needing to stop because of intense pain and feeling that the knee may "give out." She has had two documented falls in the last three months:

1) On 2024-09-18, she fell while stepping off a curb near her home when her left knee buckled. She sustained a significant left forearm hematoma and abrasions but fortunately no fracture. This event is documented in my clinic note dated 2024-09-20 in the Providence EMR.

2) On 2024-11-02, she slipped in her kitchen while turning and reported that her left knee "locked" and then gave out. Although she did not sustain a hip fracture, she had extensive bruising over her left hip and required assistance from a neighbor and EMS lift assist to get up. Since that event, she has become extremely fearful of falling again and has started limiting her activity to the bare minimum.

Conservative treatment has been fully exhausted and is no longer effective. Over the last 18 months, she has undergone:
- Multiple courses of physical therapy focused on quadriceps strengthening and gait training (most recently from 2024-04 through 2024-07), with only transient mild improvement.
- Regular use of acetaminophen, topical diclofenac, and intermittent low-dose NSAIDs, which now provide minimal relief and are limited by her mild chronic kidney disease and history of gastritis.
- Two intra-articular corticosteroid injections (2024-02-10 and 2024-08-15) with diminishing returns — the last injection provided only about two weeks of partial symptom relief.

Her most recent weight-bearing radiographs from 2024-10-05 show severe tricompartmental osteoarthritis of the left knee with joint space narrowing to bone-on-bone contact in the medial compartment, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. The orthopedic surgeon, Dr. Laura J. McMillan, has determined that she is an appropriate candidate for elective total knee arthroplasty and that further delay will not provide additional benefit from conservative measures.

Functionally, she is now struggling with basic activities of daily living:
- She has difficulty safely navigating stairs to the laundry area in her basement and has nearly fallen multiple times.
- She avoids showering on some days because stepping into the tub feels unstable, raising concern for a potentially catastrophic fall.
- She can no longer stand long enough to cook full meals, leading her to rely more on processed and microwave foods, which is negatively impacting her nutritional status and weight.

Given her age, comorbidities, and documented history of recent falls, additional delays in surgical intervention significantly increase her risk of a serious fall-related injury such as a hip fracture or head trauma. Such outcomes would likely require prolonged hospitalization, rehabilitation, and potentially long-term institutional care, which would be far more costly and detrimental to her quality of life than proceeding with timely knee replacement surgery.

For these reasons, I am requesting expedited review so that we can proceed with surgery on or near 2025-01-20 as scheduled, minimizing the window of time in which she remains at high risk for a disabling fall and further functional decline.

in_network_benefits.explanation_required_in_network_benefits:
All requested services, including the orthopedic consultation, planned total knee arthroplasty, inpatient hospital stay, and subsequent outpatient physical therapy, are to be performed within the Providence network. The requesting provider (Dr. Daniel R. Kim), the orthopedic surgeon (Dr. Laura J. McMillan), and the servicing facility (Providence Portland Medical Center) are all contracted in-network providers/facilities with Providence Medicare Advantage. There is no need for out-of-network coverage for this case, and no out-of-network providers are being requested.

This authorization request is being submitted to ensure appropriate pre-approval for the in-network inpatient admission, surgical procedure, related imaging, and a defined course of post-operative physical therapy visits under the member’s current Providence Medicare Advantage plan.

Over the past 6 months, Ms. Martha Henderson has experienced a marked decline in mobility and independence secondary to severe osteoarthritis of the left knee. She now reports that she can walk only about half a block, even with a cane, before needing to stop because of intense pain and feeling that the knee may "give out." She has had two documented falls in the last three months:

1) On 2024-09-18, she fell while stepping off a curb near her home when her left knee buckled. She sustained a significant left forearm hematoma and abrasions but fortunately no fracture. This event is documented in my clinic note dated 2024-09-20 in the Providence EMR.

2) On 2024-11-02, she slipped in her kitchen while turning and reported that her left knee "locked" and then gave out. Although she did not sustain a hip fracture, she had extensive bruising over her left hip and required assistance from a neighbor and EMS lift assist to get up. Since that event, she has become extremely fearful of falling again and has started limiting her activity to the bare minimum.

Conservative treatment has been fully exhausted and is no longer effective. Over the last 18 months, she has undergone:
- Multiple courses of physical therapy focused on quadriceps strengthening and gait training (most recently from 2024-04 through 2024-07), with only transient mild improvement.
- Regular use of acetaminophen, topical diclofenac, and intermittent low-dose NSAIDs, which now provide minimal relief and are limited by her mild chronic kidney disease and history of gastritis.
- Two intra-articular corticosteroid injections (2024-02-10 and 2024-08-15) with diminishing returns — the last injection provided only about two weeks of partial symptom relief.

Her most recent weight-bearing radiographs from 2024-10-05 show severe tricompartmental osteoarthritis of the left knee with joint space narrowing to bone-on-bone contact in the medial compartment, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. The orthopedic surgeon, Dr. Laura J. McMillan, has determined that she is an appropriate candidate for elective total knee arthroplasty and that further delay will not provide additional benefit from conservative measures.

Functionally, she is now struggling with basic activities of daily living:
- She has difficulty safely navigating stairs to the laundry area in her basement and has nearly fallen multiple times.
- She avoids showering on some days because stepping into the tub feels unstable, raising concern for a potentially catastrophic fall.
- She can no longer stand long enough to cook full meals, leading her to rely more on processed and microwave foods, which is negatively impacting her nutritional status and weight.

Given her age, comorbidities, and documented history of recent falls, additional delays in surgical intervention significantly increase her risk of a serious fall-related injury such as a hip fracture or head trauma. Such outcomes would likely require prolonged hospitalization, rehabilitation, and potentially long-term institutional care, which would be far more costly and detrimental to her quality of life than proceeding with timely knee replacement surgery.

For these reasons, I am requesting expedited review so that we can proceed with surgery on or near 2025-01-20 as scheduled, minimizing the window of time in which she remains at high risk for a disabling fall and further functional decline.

experience___position_1.name_and_address_of_employer:
Evergreen Data Solutions, Inc.
1458 Market Street, Suite 900
San Francisco, CA 94103
experience___position_1.type_of_business: Information technology consulting and data analytics services
experience___position_1.describe_your_duties_in_detail:
In my role as Senior Data Analyst at Evergreen Data Solutions, I serve as the primary point of contact for several mid-size clients in the healthcare and retail sectors, helping them translate messy, real-world operational data into actionable insights. On a day-to-day basis, I design and maintain data pipelines, build dashboards, and work closely with both technical and non-technical stakeholders to ensure our analyses actually answer the underlying business questions rather than just producing attractive charts.

Core responsibilities:
- Lead end-to-end analytics projects from requirements gathering through delivery. This includes meeting with client leadership (often CFOs, operations managers, and product leads) to clarify goals, defining success metrics, and then translating those into technical specifications for our internal engineering team.
- Extract, clean, and transform data from multiple sources (including SQL databases, CSV exports from legacy systems, and third-party APIs). I use SQL extensively (PostgreSQL and MySQL), as well as Python (primarily pandas, NumPy, and Jupyter notebooks) for more complex data wrangling and exploratory analysis.
- Build and maintain interactive dashboards in Tableau and Power BI that are used by client executives for weekly and monthly reporting. For example, for a regional healthcare network client, I designed a patient throughput dashboard that breaks down wait times by facility, time of day, and physician, which they now use in their quarterly operational review meetings.
- Perform statistical analyses and A/B test evaluations to guide client decisions. One concrete example: for a retail client testing different email marketing subject lines, I designed the experiment, calculated required sample sizes, monitored performance, and ultimately demonstrated a statistically significant 12% increase in open rates for a new subject-line strategy.

Knowledge, skills, and abilities required and used:
- Advanced proficiency in SQL, including writing complex joins, window functions, and optimizing queries for performance on large tables (tens of millions of rows).
- Strong working knowledge of Python for data analysis, including building reproducible analysis scripts and using virtual environments and Git for version control. I frequently review pull requests from junior analysts to ensure code quality and maintainability.
- Ability to communicate complex analytical findings in plain language. I regularly present to non-technical client stakeholders using slide decks I create in Google Slides or PowerPoint, focusing on the “so what” of the data rather than just the numbers.
- Practical understanding of statistics (confidence intervals, regression, hypothesis testing) and the pitfalls of misinterpreting data (e.g., selection bias, survivorship bias). I often find myself pushing back gently when a client wants to draw a conclusion that the data doesn’t fully support.

Leadership and supervision:
- Supervise and mentor a small team of 2–3 junior analysts on rotating projects. This includes reviewing their work, providing detailed written and verbal feedback, and helping them plan their professional development (e.g., recommending courses and certifications).
- Coordinate with our data engineering team to prioritize feature requests, such as creating new derived tables or adding new data sources to our warehouse. I often act as a bridge between client needs and engineering constraints.

Key accomplishments:
- Led a 9-month engagement with a multi-clinic healthcare provider that resulted in a documented 18% reduction in average patient wait times. I designed the baseline measurement approach, helped implement a new triage tracking system, and built the monitoring dashboards that leadership still uses.
- Developed a standardized reporting template and internal documentation for new retail analytics clients, which reduced onboarding time for new projects by approximately 25%. This included a written data dictionary, SQL query templates, and a checklist of common data quality issues to test for.
- Served as the primary analytics contact during the client’s transition to a new electronic health record (EHR) system. I helped map old data fields to the new schema, identified several data integrity issues early, and prevented what could have been a major reporting outage during the transition.

Overall, this role has required a mix of technical depth, patience, and diplomacy. A big part of my job is setting realistic expectations with clients about what their data can and cannot tell them, while still finding creative ways to uncover useful insights. I often stay late before major client presentations to double-check numbers and ensure that any assumptions and limitations are clearly documented in the deliverables.
experience___position_1.reason_for_leaving: Relocating to the Seattle area to be closer to family and pursuing an opportunity that is more focused on in-house analytics within a single organization rather than consulting across multiple clients. I have discussed my move with my current supervisor and provided over two months’ notice to ensure a smooth transition of my projects and proper handoff of documentation and client relationships.

Bayview Community Health Center
3270 Mission Street
San Francisco, CA 94110
experience___position_2.immediate_supervisors_2: Dr. Michael Alvarez, Director of Operations
experience___position_2.describe_your_duties_in_detail_2:
At Bayview Community Health Center, a nonprofit clinic serving primarily low-income and uninsured patients, I was responsible for designing, maintaining, and improving the clinic’s data and reporting systems. Because we were a relatively small organization with a limited IT budget, my role was a blend of data analyst, ad hoc database administrator, and internal trainer for staff who needed to use our reports.

Core responsibilities:
- Managed monthly and quarterly clinical and operational reports required by funders, including HRSA (Health Resources and Services Administration) and several private foundations. This involved pulling data from our electronic health record (EHR) system, cleaning it in Excel and SQL, and then compiling and formatting reports according to each funder’s specific templates.
- Created internal performance dashboards that tracked key metrics such as appointment no-show rates, chronic disease management indicators (e.g., percentage of diabetic patients with controlled A1C), and patient satisfaction scores. Initially these were mostly Excel-based, but over time I migrated the most important ones to Google Data Studio to make them easier for leadership to access and interpret.
- Worked closely with the front-desk and nursing staff to improve data quality. For example, I noticed that our insurance status field was often left blank or coded inconsistently, which affected our funding reports. I organized a short training session and created a one-page reference guide with screenshots to show exactly how to enter and verify this information in the EHR.
- Helped design and track quality improvement (QI) initiatives. One specific project involved reducing missed appointments. I pulled historical data to identify patterns (for example, higher no-show rates on Monday mornings and among new patients), then worked with the operations team to pilot reminder calls and text messages. I set up a simple pre/post analysis to measure the effect and documented a 9% relative reduction in no-show rates over six months.

Knowledge, skills, and abilities required and used:
- Proficient use of Excel (including pivot tables, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, and basic macros) to clean, summarize, and visualize data for staff who were more comfortable in spreadsheets than in specialized analytics tools.
- Working knowledge of SQL to query the EHR’s reporting database. I frequently wrote queries that joined patient, encounter, and billing tables to answer questions from leadership such as “How many unique patients did we see last quarter by ZIP code?”
- Strong attention to detail and understanding of protected health information (PHI) and HIPAA privacy requirements. I was responsible for ensuring that any reports shared externally were properly de-identified when necessary and that staff understood which fields should never be emailed outside the secure system.
- Ability to explain data-related concepts to colleagues with very different levels of technical comfort. For example, I would sit down one-on-one with a clinician who wanted to understand why their panel’s blood pressure control rate looked low and walk them through how the numerator and denominator were defined.

Additional responsibilities and collaboration:
- Served as the main point of contact with our EHR vendor for reporting-related issues. When an update to the system broke one of our standard reports, I documented the problem with screenshots and sample patient IDs and worked with the vendor’s support team to resolve it.
- Assisted the grants manager by providing data for grant proposals and renewal applications. I would pull historical service volume, demographic breakdowns, and outcome measures, and then help translate those numbers into narratives that aligned with each funder’s priorities.
- Occasionally covered basic IT troubleshooting tasks (e.g., helping staff with login issues for the reporting portal or setting up new staff accounts in the EHR’s reporting module) when our part-time IT contractor was not available.

Key accomplishments:
- Designed and implemented a standardized monthly reporting package for the leadership team that consolidated financial, clinical, and operational metrics into a single workbook. Before this, each department kept their own spreadsheets, and there were frequent disagreements over “whose numbers were right.” After the change, leadership meetings became more focused on decision-making rather than debating data definitions.
- Led a data cleanup project focused on patient demographic information, which improved the accuracy of our reports to funders. By systematically identifying missing or inconsistent values and working with front-line staff to correct them at check-in, we increased the completeness of race/ethnicity fields from roughly 72% to over 93% within one year.
- Supported a successful grant renewal by helping demonstrate improved chronic disease management outcomes over a three-year period. I pulled and analyzed data on hypertension and diabetes control, created trend charts, and wrote a short narrative describing our quality improvement efforts that the grants manager incorporated directly into the application.

This role gave me a ground-level view of how data can directly affect funding, staffing decisions, and ultimately patient care in a resource-constrained environment. It also pushed me to be resourceful, since we did not always have the ideal tools or perfect data, and to build trust with colleagues who were initially skeptical of being “measured” by numbers.
experience___position_2.reason_for_leaving_2: I decided to pursue a more technically focused role with broader exposure to different industries and more advanced analytics tools. After nearly four years at Bayview, I felt I had grown as much as I could within the constraints of a small nonprofit environment and wanted to deepen my skills in SQL, Python, and data visualization by moving into a consulting position at Evergreen Data Solutions. I left on good terms, provided a full month of transition support, and documented my reporting processes so that my successor could step into the role more smoothly.

differences.ground_truth: true

Evergreen Data Solutions, Inc.
1458 Market Street, Suite 900
San Francisco, CA 94103

In my role as Senior Data Analyst at Evergreen Data Solutions, I serve as the primary point of contact for several mid-size clients in the healthcare and retail sectors, helping them translate messy, real-world operational data into actionable insights. On a day-to-day basis, I design and maintain data pipelines, build dashboards, and work closely with both technical and non-technical stakeholders to ensure our analyses actually answer the underlying business questions rather than just producing attractive charts.

Core responsibilities:
- Lead end-to-end analytics projects from requirements gathering through delivery. This includes meeting with client leadership (often CFOs, operations managers, and product leads) to clarify goals, defining success metrics, and then translating those into technical specifications for our internal engineering team.
- Extract, clean, and transform data from multiple sources (including SQL databases, CSV exports from legacy systems, and third-party APIs). I use SQL extensively (PostgreSQL and MySQL), as well as Python (primarily pandas, NumPy, and Jupyter notebooks) for more complex data wrangling and exploratory analysis.
- Build and maintain interactive dashboards in Tableau and Power BI that are used by client executives for weekly and monthly reporting. For example, for a regional healthcare network client, I designed a patient throughput dashboard that breaks down wait times by facility, time of day, and physician, which they now use in their quarterly operational review meetings.
- Perform statistical analyses and A/B test evaluations to guide client decisions. One concrete example: for a retail client testing different email marketing subject lines, I designed the experiment, calculated required sample sizes, monitored performance, and ultimately demonstrated a statistically significant 12% increase in open rates for a new subject-line strategy.

Knowledge, skills, and abilities required and used:
- Advanced proficiency in SQL, including writing complex joins, window functions, and optimizing queries for performance on large tables (tens of millions of rows).
- Strong working knowledge of Python for data analysis, including building reproducible analysis scripts and using virtual environments and Git for version control. I frequently review pull requests from junior analysts to ensure code quality and maintainability.
- Ability to communicate complex analytical findings in plain language. I regularly present to non-technical client stakeholders using slide decks I create in Google Slides or PowerPoint, focusing on the “so what” of the data rather than just the numbers.
- Practical understanding of statistics (confidence intervals, regression, hypothesis testing) and the pitfalls of misinterpreting data (e.g., selection bias, survivorship bias). I often find myself pushing back gently when a client wants to draw a conclusion that the data doesn’t fully support.

Leadership and supervision:
- Supervise and mentor a small team of 2–3 junior analysts on rotating projects. This includes reviewing their work, providing detailed written and verbal feedback, and helping them plan their professional development (e.g., recommending courses and certifications).
- Coordinate with our data engineering team to prioritize feature requests, such as creating new derived tables or adding new data sources to our warehouse. I often act as a bridge between client needs and engineering constraints.

Key accomplishments:
- Led a 9-month engagement with a multi-clinic healthcare provider that resulted in a documented 18% reduction in average patient wait times. I designed the baseline measurement approach, helped implement a new triage tracking system, and built the monitoring dashboards that leadership still uses.
- Developed a standardized reporting template and internal documentation for new retail analytics clients, which reduced onboarding time for new projects by approximately 25%. This included a written data dictionary, SQL query templates, and a checklist of common data quality issues to test for.
- Served as the primary analytics contact during the client’s transition to a new electronic health record (EHR) system. I helped map old data fields to the new schema, identified several data integrity issues early, and prevented what could have been a major reporting outage during the transition.

Overall, this role has required a mix of technical depth, patience, and diplomacy. A big part of my job is setting realistic expectations with clients about what their data can and cannot tell them, while still finding creative ways to uncover useful insights. I often stay late before major client presentations to double-check numbers and ensure that any assumptions and limitations are clearly documented in the deliverables.

differences.ground_truth:
At Bayview Community Health Center, a nonprofit clinic serving primarily low-income and uninsured patients, I was responsible for designing, maintaining, and improving the clinic’s data and reporting systems. Because we were a relatively small organization with a limited IT budget, my role was a blend of data analyst, ad hoc database administrator, and internal trainer for staff who needed to use our reports.

Core responsibilities:
- Managed monthly and quarterly clinical and operational reports required by funders, including HRSA (Health Resources and Services Administration) and several private foundations. This involved pulling data from our electronic health record (EHR) system, cleaning it in Excel and SQL, and then compiling and formatting reports according to each funder’s specific templates.
- Created internal performance dashboards that tracked key metrics such as appointment no-show rates, chronic disease management indicators (e.g., percentage of diabetic patients with controlled A1C), and patient satisfaction scores. Initially these were mostly Excel-based, but over time I migrated the most important ones to Google Data Studio to make them easier for leadership to access and interpret.
- Worked closely with the front-desk and nursing staff to improve data quality. For example, I noticed that our insurance status field was often left blank or coded inconsistently, which affected our funding reports. I organized a short training session and created a one-page reference guide with screenshots to show exactly how to enter and verify this information in the EHR.
- Helped design and track quality improvement (QI) initiatives. One specific project involved reducing missed appointments. I pulled historical data to identify patterns (for example, higher no-show rates on Monday mornings and among new patients), then worked with the operations team to pilot reminder calls and text messages. I set up a simple pre/post analysis to measure the effect and documented a 9% relative reduction in no-show rates over six months.

Knowledge, skills, and abilities required and used:
- Proficient use of Excel (including pivot tables, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, and basic macros) to clean, summarize, and visualize data for staff who were more comfortable in spreadsheets than in specialized analytics tools.
- Working knowledge of SQL to query the EHR’s reporting database. I frequently wrote queries that joined patient, encounter, and billing tables to answer questions from leadership such as “How many unique patients did we see last quarter by ZIP code?”
- Strong attention to detail and understanding of protected health information (PHI) and HIPAA privacy requirements. I was responsible for ensuring that any reports shared externally were properly de-identified when necessary and that staff understood which fields should never be emailed outside the secure system.
- Ability to explain data-related concepts to colleagues with very different levels of technical comfort. For example, I would sit down one-on-one with a clinician who wanted to understand why their panel’s blood pressure control rate looked low and walk them through how the numerator and denominator were defined.

Additional responsibilities and collaboration:
- Served as the main point of contact with our EHR vendor for reporting-related issues. When an update to the system broke one of our standard reports, I documented the problem with screenshots and sample patient IDs and worked with the vendor’s support team to resolve it.
- Assisted the grants manager by providing data for grant proposals and renewal applications. I would pull historical service volume, demographic breakdowns, and outcome measures, and then help translate those numbers into narratives that aligned with each funder’s priorities.
- Occasionally covered basic IT troubleshooting tasks (e.g., helping staff with login issues for the reporting portal or setting up new staff accounts in the EHR’s reporting module) when our part-time IT contractor was not available.

Key accomplishments:
- Designed and implemented a standardized monthly reporting package for the leadership team that consolidated financial, clinical, and operational metrics into a single workbook. Before this, each department kept their own spreadsheets, and there were frequent disagreements over “whose numbers were right.” After the change, leadership meetings became more focused on decision-making rather than debating data definitions.
- Led a data cleanup project focused on patient demographic information, which improved the accuracy of our reports to funders. By systematically identifying missing or inconsistent values and working with front-line staff to correct them at check-in, we increased the completeness of race/ethnicity fields from roughly 72% to over 93% within one year.
- Supported a successful grant renewal by helping demonstrate improved chronic disease management outcomes over a three-year period. I pulled and analyzed data on hypertension and diabetes control, created trend charts, and wrote a short narrative describing our quality improvement efforts that the grants manager incorporated directly into the application.

This role gave me a ground-level view of how data can directly affect funding, staffing decisions, and ultimately patient care in a resource-constrained environment. It also pushed me to be resourceful, since we did not always have the ideal tools or perfect data, and to build trust with colleagues who were initially skeptical of being “measured” by numbers.

differences.ground_truth: Nonprofit community healthcare clinic

I decided to pursue a more technically focused role with broader exposure to different industries and more advanced analytics tools. After nearly four years at Bayview, I felt I had grown as much as I could within the constraints of a small nonprofit environment and wanted to deepen my skills in SQL, Python, and data visualization by moving into a consulting position at Evergreen Data Solutions. I left on good terms, provided a full month of transition support, and documented my reporting processes so that my successor could step into the role more smoothly.

meeting__agenda_details.title_of_item: Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet

submitter__presenter_information.presenter_name_title: Michael R. Anderson, Public Works Director

issue_details.summary_of_issue: The issue involves replacing Unit 23, an aging snowplow truck, to avoid operational disruptions and potential safety risks.
issue_details.alternatives_options_effects_on_others_c: Alternatives include deferring the purchase or buying used, but these are not recommended due to past issues with Unit 23.
issue_details.recommended_action_motion: Authorize the purchase of a new snowplow truck.

financial_impact.please_explain: The cost is part of the 2025 Capital Improvement Plan, and delaying the purchase could affect future budgets.

meeting__agenda_details.title_of_item: Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet

submitter__presenter_information.presenter_name_title: Michael R. Anderson, Public Works Director

issue_details.summary_of_issue:
The Public Works Department – Streets Division is requesting authorization to purchase one (1) replacement tandem-axle snowplow truck with front plow, wing plow, and underbody scraper to replace Unit #23, a 2006 International 7600 snowplow that has reached the end of its reliable service life.

Background and Current Situation:
Unit #23 has 167,000 miles and approximately 11,400 engine hours, the majority of which are high-stress winter maintenance hours involving heavy plowing and salting operations. Over the past three winter seasons, the vehicle has experienced increasing downtime due to major mechanical and corrosion-related issues, including:
- Frequent hydraulic system leaks (hoses and fittings) due to corrosion
- Frame and dump box rust, including several areas that have required patching and reinforcement
- Repeated electrical problems, particularly in the lighting and control systems for the plows and spreader
- Transmission slipping under load last season, which required a significant repair and temporary removal from service during a major storm event

During the 2023–2024 winter season, Unit #23 was out of service for a total of 17 working days due to breakdowns. When a plow truck is down during a storm, the City must either reassign routes—leading to longer plow cycle times and delayed service for residents—or pay additional overtime to keep other trucks out longer. In one January 2024 storm, the unplanned downtime contributed to several residential streets not being opened curb-to-curb until nearly 24 hours after the snowfall ended, resulting in a noticeable increase in resident complaints and calls to City Hall.

The City’s adopted Equipment Replacement Plan identifies a typical service life of 12–15 years for tandem-axle plow trucks under Brainerd’s operating conditions. Unit #23 is now nearly 19 years old. Our fleet currently consists of six primary route plow trucks; losing the reliability of even one truck has a significant impact on our ability to meet our established level-of-service standards (first pass on priority routes within 6 hours of storm end, and residential streets within 12–18 hours, depending on severity).

To maintain public safety, control long-term maintenance costs, and ensure consistent winter operations, staff are recommending replacement of Unit #23 with a new 2025 model tandem-axle truck chassis equipped with a stainless steel dump body, front plow, wing plow, underbody scraper, and pre-wet salt application system. This configuration matches our current standard specifications and is compatible with our existing maintenance practices and parts inventories.

We have obtained a quote through the State of Minnesota Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV) program, which allows the City to purchase at pre-bid, volume-discounted pricing without conducting a separate formal bid. The proposed vendor is Nuss Truck & Equipment for the chassis, with the plow and body package to be supplied and installed by Towmaster Truck Equipment. The combined quote, including all required equipment, installation, and delivery, is $298,450.27, which is slightly below the $305,000 amount programmed in the 2025 Capital Improvement Plan for this replacement.

In addition to addressing the deteriorated condition of Unit #23, the new truck will include several safety and efficiency improvements:
- Enhanced LED lighting package for better visibility in low-visibility winter conditions
- Onboard pre-wet system to reduce overall salt usage and associated environmental impacts
- Improved operator ergonomics, including better seat positioning, controls layout, and camera system for backing and wing operation

This agenda item seeks Council approval to move forward with the purchase as outlined in the attached quote and to authorize the Public Works Director and City Administrator to execute the necessary purchase documents.
issue_details.alternatives_options_effects_on_others_c:
Alternatives and Options Considered:
1. Defer Replacement for 1–2 Years:
   One alternative considered was to extend the service life of Unit #23 by an additional 1–2 winter seasons. Staff and our fleet mechanic evaluated this option last spring. To keep the truck in minimally reliable condition, we would need to perform significant rust remediation on the frame and dump body, replace several hydraulic lines and fittings pre-emptively, and potentially invest in a partial transmission overhaul. Our mechanic estimates this would cost in the range of $18,000–$25,000 in parts and labor over the next two years, with no guarantee that other major components (e.g., engine, differential, electrical harness) would not fail. Furthermore, even with these investments, the truck would likely continue to experience unplanned downtime, especially during heavy-use periods. This option was not recommended due to poor long-term value and increased risk to service reliability.

2. Purchase a Used Plow Truck:
   We also briefly examined the used equipment market. Recent experience and contacts with neighboring cities suggest that lightly used municipal plow trucks are difficult to find and often come with unknown maintenance histories. Prices for 5–7 year old used plow trucks with similar equipment have been in the $180,000–$220,000 range, with significantly shorter remaining service life and no manufacturer warranty. The risk of inheriting another vehicle with corrosion or hidden issues is high. For these reasons, staff concluded that a used truck does not provide a meaningful long-term cost advantage compared to a new unit with full warranty coverage and a 15-year expected service life.

3. Outsource Plowing of One Route:
   Another theoretical option would be to contract out one of our plow routes to a private contractor rather than replacing the truck. While some communities do supplement with private contractors during extreme events, relying on a contractor for a full route every storm would introduce scheduling and coordination challenges. Based on informal quotes obtained in 2022 for emergency backup plowing, we estimate the seasonal cost to contract a full route could approach $60,000–$80,000 per winter, depending on snowfall. Over a 10–15 year horizon, this would likely exceed the cost of owning and operating our own equipment. Additionally, we would have reduced direct control over operator training, response times, and adherence to our level-of-service standards.

4. Reduce Level of Service Standards:
   Council could choose to formally reduce the City’s winter maintenance standards, such as allowing longer plow cycle times or lower priority for certain residential streets, thereby potentially operating with fewer reliable plow units. Staff does not recommend this option. Consistently passable streets are a core public safety function, particularly for emergency response, school transportation, and access for residents who must travel to work during and immediately after storms. Lowering the standard could also increase liability exposure in the event of accidents where road conditions are a factor.

Effects on Others:
- Residents and Businesses: Replacing the truck will help maintain or slightly improve snow and ice control response times, especially during multi-day or back-to-back storm events. This is particularly important for residents living on hills and cul-de-sacs, who are more vulnerable to slippery conditions when plowing is delayed.
- Public Works Staff: Operators have expressed concern about the reliability and safety of Unit #23. In the past two winters, there have been multiple instances where the truck lost hydraulic pressure while the wing was extended or where lighting failures reduced visibility during nighttime operations. The new truck’s improved ergonomics and safety features will reduce operator fatigue and stress during long shifts.
- Environment: The inclusion of a modern pre-wet system and more precise salt application controls aligns with the City’s goal of reducing chloride usage and protecting local waterways. Staff anticipate a modest but measurable reduction in salt consumption on the route served by this truck, based on experience from newer trucks purchased in 2019 and 2021.
- Budget and Taxpayers: While the upfront cost is significant, this replacement has been planned in the City’s Capital Improvement Plan and Equipment Replacement Fund for several years. Proceeding now avoids the risk of substantial unplanned repair expenses on a vehicle that is already beyond its intended service life. Over the long term, maintaining a consistent replacement schedule helps smooth out capital costs and avoids spikes in any single budget year.

Comments:
This request has been reviewed by the Streets Superintendent, the City’s fleet mechanic, and the Finance Director. All concur that the timing is appropriate and that the State CPV pricing represents a competitive value. The proposed truck specifications match those of our last two plow truck purchases, which have performed well, allowing for some standardization of parts and operator training. Staff is mindful of broader economic conditions, including vehicle cost inflation; delaying the purchase further may expose the City to additional price increases in future model years.

In summary, while there are theoretical alternatives, each carries greater operational risk, long-term cost, or service-level impacts compared to the recommended purchase. Staff believes proceeding with the planned replacement is the most responsible and sustainable option for the community.
issue_details.recommended_action_motion: Motion to adopt a resolution authorizing the purchase of one (1) 2025 model tandem-axle snowplow truck, including chassis, stainless steel dump body, front plow, wing plow, underbody scraper, and pre-wet salt application system, from Nuss Truck & Equipment and Towmaster Truck Equipment through the State of Minnesota Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV) program in the total amount of $298,450.27, plus applicable title and licensing fees; and to authorize the Public Works Director and City Administrator to execute all necessary purchase documents, with funding to be provided from the Equipment Replacement Fund as programmed in the 2025 Capital Improvement Plan.

financial_impact.please_explain:
The total estimated cost for the purchase of the replacement snowplow truck, including chassis, plow and body package, installation, and delivery, is $298,450.27. This figure is based on formal written quotes obtained through the State of Minnesota Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV) program from Nuss Truck & Equipment (for the truck chassis) and Towmaster Truck Equipment (for the plow, dump body, underbody scraper, and pre-wet system). The CPV pricing already reflects competitive, pre-bid volume discounts. Sales tax does not apply to this purchase because the City, as a governmental entity, is tax-exempt on qualifying equipment purchases under Minnesota law. Title, licensing, and minor upfitting costs (such as City decals and radio installation) are expected to be minimal and will be covered within the existing budget authority for this project.

This replacement was anticipated and programmed in the City’s 2025 Capital Improvement Plan (CIP) under the Public Works – Streets Division equipment schedule. The CIP identifies a budget of $305,000 for the replacement of Unit #23, funded through the Equipment Replacement Fund. That fund is supported by annual internal service charges assessed to operating departments based on projected equipment lifecycles and replacement costs. Over the past several years, the Streets Division has been making regular contributions to this fund specifically for plow truck replacements, including this unit.

Because the quoted amount of $298,450.27 is approximately $6,500 below the programmed budget, no additional appropriation or budget amendment is required. The slight underrun provides a small contingency to cover any unforeseen minor costs associated with delivery, inspection, or small modifications needed to integrate the new truck into our existing fleet (for example, adapting radio mounts or adding a specific tool storage configuration requested by operators).

Ongoing operating costs (fuel, routine maintenance, and insurance) for the new unit are expected to be comparable to or slightly lower than the current truck, particularly in the first several years when major repairs should be minimal and warranty coverage is in effect. In contrast, if the City were to retain Unit #23, we would likely face escalating repair costs. Over the last three budget years, we have spent an average of approximately $9,300 per year on repairs for Unit #23, not including routine oil changes and inspections. These repair costs are expected to increase further if the vehicle remains in service.

There is no projected impact on the City’s property tax levy beyond what has already been incorporated into the long-term equipment replacement funding model. The Finance Director has reviewed this request and confirmed that sufficient funds are available in the Equipment Replacement Fund to support the purchase without affecting other planned 2025 capital projects.

In summary, the financial impact is a planned, budgeted capital expenditure that is already accounted for in the City’s long-range financial planning. The purchase should help avoid higher unplanned repair costs and potential overtime expenditures associated with breakdowns, thereby supporting more predictable operating budgets in future years.

differences.ground_truth:
Alternatives and Options Considered:
1. Defer Replacement for 1–2 Years:
   One alternative considered was to extend the service life of Unit #23 by an additional 1–2 winter seasons. Staff and our fleet mechanic evaluated this option last spring. To keep the truck in minimally reliable condition, we would need to perform significant rust remediation on the frame and dump body, replace several hydraulic lines and fittings pre-emptively, and potentially invest in a partial transmission overhaul. Our mechanic estimates this would cost in the range of $18,000–$25,000 in parts and labor over the next two years, with no guarantee that other major components (e.g., engine, differential, electrical harness) would not fail. Furthermore, even with these investments, the truck would likely continue to experience unplanned downtime, especially during heavy-use periods. This option was not recommended due to poor long-term value and increased risk to service reliability.

2. Purchase a Used Plow Truck:
   We also briefly examined the used equipment market. Recent experience and contacts with neighboring cities suggest that lightly used municipal plow trucks are difficult to find and often come with unknown maintenance histories. Prices for 5–7 year old used plow trucks with similar equipment have been in the $180,000–$220,000 range, with significantly shorter remaining service life and no manufacturer warranty. The risk of inheriting another vehicle with corrosion or hidden issues is high. For these reasons, staff concluded that a used truck does not provide a meaningful long-term cost advantage compared to a new unit with full warranty coverage and a 15-year expected service life.

3. Outsource Plowing of One Route:
   Another theoretical option would be to contract out one of our plow routes to a private contractor rather than replacing the truck. While some communities do supplement with private contractors during extreme events, relying on a contractor for a full route every storm would introduce scheduling and coordination challenges. Based on informal quotes obtained in 2022 for emergency backup plowing, we estimate the seasonal cost to contract a full route could approach $60,000–$80,000 per winter, depending on snowfall. Over a 10–15 year horizon, this would likely exceed the cost of owning and operating our own equipment. Additionally, we would have reduced direct control over operator training, response times, and adherence to our level-of-service standards.

Staff does not recommend this option. Lowering the standard could also increase liability exposure in the event of accidents where road conditions are a factor.
